CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is generally well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early, making the high-level goals and experimental setup clear. Notation and methodological descriptions appear consistent enough to follow, and figures/tables/examples are reported as effective; the writing quality is described as good and related work coverage adequate. From my perspective, the methods section could provide clearer, more precise description of the test-time model modification and stimulus construction (to match the rigor expected for interpretability claims), and some presentation of experimental details (e.g., hyperparameters) is missing which mildly hampers reproducibility and clarity. | B(4): Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early (novel interpretability method, LSTM application, correlation validation). Writing quality is reported as good and related work coverage is adequate, so the flow from method to experiments to conclusion appears logical. Some presentation gaps remain for reproducibility (hyperparameters not reported, code not mentioned), and the validation relies on modest correlations (.32–.43) that could use clearer discussion and more informative figures/tables to strengthen interpretation for domain readers. From an applied interpretability perspective the paper is clear and usable but would benefit from a few additional methodological details and visual summaries.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is generally well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early, making the high-level goals and experimental setup clear. Notation and methodological descriptions appear consistent enough to follow, and figures/tables/examples are reported as effective; the writing quality is described as good and related work coverage adequate. From my perspective, the methods section could provide clearer, more precise description of the test-time model modification and stimulus construction (to match the rigor expected for interpretability claims), and some presentation of experimental details (e.g., hyperparameters) is missing which mildly hampers reproducibility and clarity.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early (novel interpretability method, LSTM application, correlation validation). Writing quality is reported as good and related work coverage is adequate, so the flow from method to experiments to conclusion appears logical. Some presentation gaps remain for reproducibility (hyperparameters not reported, code not mentioned), and the validation relies on modest correlations (.32–.43) that could use clearer discussion and more informative figures/tables to strengthen interpretation for domain readers. From an applied interpretability perspective the paper is clear and usable but would benefit from a few additional methodological details and visual summaries.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper frames interpretation as an input–output probing procedure and applies carefully constructed stimuli to map LSTM output changes to token-level features — a clear, pragmatic framing but not a fundamentally new theoretical approach compared with existing probing/occlusion and stimulus-response techniques. The empirical application to values-affirmation essays and the comparison to an SVM baseline give useful demonstration, but reliance on a single model class (LSTM), a single baseline, and correlational validation (Pearson .32–.43) indicate an incremental rather than groundbreaking advance. From a theory-focused perspective the work lacks formal identifiability or robustness guarantees and so reads as a non-trivial but modest extension of prior interpretability practices that would inform but not surprise experts. | B(3): The paper proposes a pragmatic interpretability procedure—treating an LSTM as an input-output device and querying it with constructed stimuli to map token-level probability shifts—that is a useful empirical approach but conceptually close to existing probing and intervention-based attribution techniques. The authors apply this to a novel educational dataset and compare model-derived signals to SVM coefficients (Pearson r ≈ .32–.43), which provides empirical validation but indicates only partial agreement with standard baselines. From my applied interpretability perspective, the method is a non-trivial, practically oriented extension rather than a radically new paradigm, so it represents a moderate (incremental) novelty.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper frames interpretation as an input–output probing procedure and applies carefully constructed stimuli to map LSTM output changes to token-level features — a clear, pragmatic framing but not a fundamentally new theoretical approach compared with existing probing/occlusion and stimulus-response techniques. The empirical application to values-affirmation essays and the comparison to an SVM baseline give useful demonstration, but reliance on a single model class (LSTM), a single baseline, and correlational validation (Pearson .32–.43) indicate an incremental rather than groundbreaking advance. From a theory-focused perspective the work lacks formal identifiability or robustness guarantees and so reads as a non-trivial but modest extension of prior interpretability practices that would inform but not surprise experts.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper proposes a pragmatic interpretability procedure—treating an LSTM as an input-output device and querying it with constructed stimuli to map token-level probability shifts—that is a useful empirical approach but conceptually close to existing probing and intervention-based attribution techniques. The authors apply this to a novel educational dataset and compare model-derived signals to SVM coefficients (Pearson r ≈ .32–.43), which provides empirical validation but indicates only partial agreement with standard baselines. From my applied interpretability perspective, the method is a non-trivial, practically oriented extension rather than a radically new paradigm, so it represents a moderate (incremental) novelty.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper states clear claims and presents reasonable experiments showing an LSTM outperforms an SVM and that token-level interpretation signals correlate modestly with SVM coefficients. However, important methodological gaps (missing hyperparameters/code), limited validation (only modest correlations, single baseline, no ablations or interventions), and lack of robustness checks mean the evidence does not fully justify stronger soundness/correctness confidence, so a mid-level score is appropriate.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper stakes clear empirical claims and provides experiments (LSTM trained on 6,224 essays vs an SVM baseline) that partially support them, but from a theory-purist perspective the work lacks formal guarantees about identifiability or robustness of the proposed stimulus-querying interpretation method. Validation rests on modest Pearson correlations (.32–.43) to SVM token coefficients and a single baseline, which is insufficient to rule out confounds (e.g., prompt artifacts, demographic leakage) or to establish causal links between tokens and predictions. Important controls are missing (no reported hyperparameters, no multiple model/ablation checks, no randomized interventions), so while conclusions are consistent with hypotheses they are not fully justified by the presented evidence.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and supported by experiments showing an LSTM that outperforms an SVM and by token-level analyses whose probability shifts correlate with SVM coefficients (Pearson r = .32–.43), providing reasonable empirical validation of the interpretation method. However, methodological reporting omits hyperparameters and code availability, and the validation is limited to modest correlations without reported statistical tests, which are minor but relevant gaps for reproducibility and strength of evidence. Overall the experimental approach and comparison to a sensible baseline are sound, but the limited quantitative agreement and reporting omissions prevent a top score.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the empirical demonstration is reasonable but modest (Pearson ≈ .32–.43) and that key reproducibility and robustness details—hyperparameters, ablations, sensitivity analyses, and code—are missing. Given the practical contribution of linking LSTM token-level shifts to SVM coefficients but limited experimental depth and unaddressed confounds, a middle rating reflects useful but not fully convincing substance.

reviewer_a.justification: The experimental setup is limited: a single LSTM vs one SVM baseline and modest Pearson correlations (.32–.43) are insufficient to support broad interpretive claims without stronger theoretical justification or multiple control models. There are no reported ablations or sensitivity analyses, hyperparameters are not provided, and code availability is unspecified, which together harm reproducibility and robustness checks I demand as a theory-oriented reviewer. Related work coverage is described as adequate and the authors note limitations and modest support, but important confounds (e.g., demographic leakage, prompt artifacts) and formal identifiability/robustness guarantees are not addressed.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a reasonable empirical demonstration (LSTM on 6,224 essays, clear baseline SVM comparison) and a pragmatic validation linking LSTM token-level probability shifts to SVM coefficients (Pearson r ≈ .32–.43), which supports the interpretation claims but only modestly. However, key reproducibility details (no hyperparameters or code reported) and a lack of reported ablations/sensitivity analyses leave important questions about robustness and alternative explanations. Related work and limitations are adequately discussed, but the experimental depth is adequate rather than comprehensive.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: Both reviewers agree the comparison to an interpretable SVM baseline on the same dataset is fair and metrics are clearly reported, and the authors candidly acknowledge limitations. However, the evaluation lacks a broader set of modern baselines, ablations, and stronger causal/robustness analyses, so the comparison is reasonable but incomplete, warranting a middle score of 3.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares the LSTM mainly to a single SVM baseline on the same essays and uses comparable metrics (e.g., token-level coefficient vs. LSTM probability shifts), so basic fairness is maintained. However, from a theory-purist perspective the reliance on one classical baseline and correlational validation (Pearsons .32–.43) is insufficient—diverse algorithmic baselines or ablation controls (≥3) and stronger causal/robustness analyses are missing. The authors do acknowledge modest support for their hypotheses and cover related work adequately, but they do not fully analyze differences with competing methods nor provide rigorous guarantees, leading me to rate the comparisons as incomplete.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares its LSTM interpretation outputs to a reasonable, interpretable baseline (SVM token coefficients) on the same values-affirmation essay dataset and uses a clear metric (Pearson correlation .32–.43) for validation, so comparisons are fair. The authors honestly acknowledge modest/partial agreement and limitations, and they discuss related work adequately. However, the evaluation omits other relevant modern baselines or interpretation methods (e.g., feature attribution techniques or alternative classifiers) and the analysis of differences beyond correlation is relatively limited, so the comparison is strong but not fully comprehensive.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper is a reasonable topical fit and contributes a practical interpretability technique validated on a sizable real-world essay corpus, but they also agree the empirical validation and methodological rigor are limited (single model, modest correlations, limited controls and reporting). Given these strengths and the substantive concerns about robustness, baselines, and reproducibility, a borderline score is appropriate—useful applied work but not yet meeting the standards for a stronger acceptability rating.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses an NLP interpretability question that is relevant to top-tier venues (analysis of LSTM behavior on a real essay dataset), but from my perspective it lacks the theoretical grounding and rigorous controls I expect for such work: the method treats the model as an I/O device without identifiability or robustness guarantees and validation relies on modest Pearson correlations (.32–.43) to an SVM baseline. Empirically it also leans on a single LSTM model with only one classical baseline rather than multiple model/ablation checks, and potential data confounds (demographic leakage, prompt artifacts) are not systematically controlled. Overall, topical fit is reasonable but methodological and validation weaknesses make it borderline for a top NLP/ML conference.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper is a good fit for a top NLP/ML venue: it introduces a practical interpretability technique for sequence models applied to a real educational corpus and compares model-derived signals against an interpretable SVM baseline (Pearson r=.32–.43). From my applied interpretability perspective I appreciate the real-data demonstration on 6,224 essays and the empirical validation, but the support is modest, code/hyperparameter details aren’t reported, and the validation relies on moderate correlations, which are scope concerns that keep it from being a perfect fit.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper addresses an important practical interpretability problem and provides useful empirical probes, but its impact is limited by modest validation (token-level correlations ~.32–.43), single-model/single-dataset evaluation, and lack of causal controls or theoretical guarantees. Given these strengths and limitations, the work is likely to spur incremental applied follow-ups rather than drive major theoretical or widespread adoption, so a moderate impact score is appropriate.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important practical interpretability problem for essay-based classifiers, but from my theory-focused perspective its impact is limited: the method lacks formal identifiability or robustness guarantees and relies on a single LSTM analysis with only one traditional baseline (SVM). Validation is modest (token-level Pearson correlations .32–.43) and the work does not convincingly control for potential confounds (demographic leakage, prompt artifacts) or provide randomized interventions, so it is unlikely to substantially change theoretical thinking or drive broad adoption beyond a niche applied setting. It may, however, seed incremental follow-up empirical work.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles an important, practical interpretability problem by proposing a simple input–output probing method for LSTMs and demonstrating it on a real educational dataset of 6,224 values-affirmation essays, which aligns with my emphasis on usable methods for domain scientists. The empirical validation (LSTM outperforming an SVM and token-level probability shifts correlating with SVM coefficients at r≈.32–.43) provides useful but only partial confirmation, limiting immediate transformative impact. The work is likely to spur follow-up applied studies in education and psychology and encourage similar stimulus-based probes, but the modest correlations, limited disentangling of causal effects, and lack of reported reproducibility assets mean its influence will be moderate rather than field-changing.

main_claims: Proposes a novel method to interpret neural networks by treating the model as an input-output device and querying it with carefully constructed stimuli to map output changes to input features.
Demonstrates an LSTM trained to recognize student gender and writing prompt (treatment vs control) on values-affirmation essays, with the LSTM outperforming a baseline classifier.
Shows that token-level probability shifts from the LSTM correlate reasonably with SVM token coefficients (Pearson correlations between .32 and .43), supporting the validity of the interpretations.
Finds evidence broadly consistent with expected gender differences in essay justifications (e.g., independence vs interdependence), providing modest support for the authors' hypotheses.
_summary.methodology: Train an LSTM to predict gender and writing instruction on values-affirmation essays, modify the model at test time to emit sequential class probabilities as each token is read, and query the model with constructed sentences/stimuli to interpret which textual features drive class predictions.
datasets: Values-affirmation essays dataset: 6224 essays written by 1233 middle school students (3315 essays by females, 2909 by males); average length 42.8 words, 5816 distinct tokens
metrics: Pearson correlation (between token SVM coefficients and LSTM average probability changes; reported correlations between .32 and .43)
limitations: Authors report only modest support for hypotheses and note that future work should disentangle the degree to which justifications influence intervention effectiveness on academic outcomes.
Validation relies on correlation between LSTM probability shifts and SVM coefficients (correlations in the .32–.43 range), indicating limited/partial agreement.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - interpretability applied to educational/psychological values-affirmation essays

reviewer_a.name: Theory Purist
reviewer_a.background: Senior researcher in machine learning theory and interpretability with a PhD in computer science; long track record of work on provable explanations and causal inference for models.
traits: penalises papers that make causal or interpretive claims based solely on correlational analyses without formal guarantees or randomized interventions
demands explicit theoretical justification or bounds for any proposed interpretability technique (e.g., identifiability, robustness to input perturbations), and deducts points if absent
reduces score for papers that rely on single-model analyses rather than ensemble/ablation checks (prefers >=3 diverse baselines/controls)
flags and penalises potential data confounds (e.g., demographic leakage, prompt artifacts) unless systematically controlled and reported
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on theoretical soundness and rigorous controls; gives low scores to empirical claims lacking formal guarantees or thorough confound analyses.

reviewer_b.name: Applied Interpretability Enthusiast
reviewer_b.background: Senior NLP researcher with experience deploying interpretability methods in social-science and education applications; publishes pragmatic empirical work that connects models to real-world decision-making.
traits: rewards papers that introduce novel, practical interpretability techniques that can be used by domain scientists, giving higher scores for clear, usable methods
rewards thorough empirical validation on real-world datasets and interdisciplinary relevance (e.g., education/psychology), and gives bonus points for insights that align with domain expectations
favours papers that compare model-derived signals to interpretable baselines (e.g., SVM coefficients) and report concrete correlation/validation metrics
gives positive adjustment when authors provide reproducibility assets (code, processed data, or clear procedures) or when the method is simple to implement
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Pragmatic, empirically-driven reviewer who prioritizes methods that produce useful, validated insights for downstream domains and values real-data demonstrations.
